name: Candle Making

seo.title: Best Beginner Candle Making Kit UK 2025 | KitUp
seo.description: Start making your own candles at home with soy wax, wicks, and fragrance oils. Beginner kit recommendations with Amazon UK links.
keywords: how to make candles at home UK

intro: Candle making is one of those crafts that looks impressive but is genuinely straightforward once you understand the basics. Melt wax, add fragrance, pour into a container, wait for it to set. Within an afternoon you can have half a dozen candles cooling on the counter, and within a few sessions you'll be dialling in your own fragrance blends and trying different wax types. The appeal is obvious: you make something practical and beautiful, your home smells wonderful, and a batch of handmade candles in nice jars makes one of the most appreciated gifts you can give. The kit requirement is minimal and most of it lives in a kitchen cupboard between sessions.

kits.label: Starter Kit
kits.description: Everything you need to make your first batch of soy wax candles at home.

products.name: Complete Candle Making Kit for Beginners
products.description: An all-in-one starter kit containing soy wax flakes, pre-tabbed cotton wicks, fragrance oils, tin or glass containers, a pouring jug, a thermometer, wick stickers, wick centring bars, and a step-by-step instruction booklet. Everything to make your first candles in one purchase.
products.why_we_picked: Sourcing every component separately is time-consuming when you're just starting and don't know what you need. A well-put-together kit gives you a matched set of materials in the right proportions: the wicks are sized for the containers, the fragrance loads are pre-tested, and the quantities let you make several candles to practice with. It also removes the early guesswork about ratios and temperatures so you can focus on learning the process rather than troubleshooting ingredients.

products.name: Golden Brands 464 Soy Wax Flakes (2kg)
products.description: Golden Brands 464 is the most widely used soy wax for container candles. It has excellent fragrance throw, a smooth finish, and good adhesion to glass jars. Sold in flake form for easy measuring and melting.
products.why_we_picked: Once your starter kit runs out, this is the wax you buy. GW464 is the industry standard for soy container candles and the wax most candle-making tutorials are written around. It melts and pours at consistent temperatures, holds fragrance oil well at the standard 10% load, and produces a clean, slightly creamy finish. Buying 2kg gives you enough to make 20–25 small candles while you experiment with different fragrance combinations.

products.name: Fragrance Oil Set (12 Scents)
products.description: A selection of 12 fragrance oils specifically formulated for candle making, covering popular scents such as vanilla, lavender, fresh linen, cedarwood, and citrus blends. Each bottle is 10ml.
products.why_we_picked: Fragrance is the whole point of a scented candle, and experimenting with blends is one of the most enjoyable parts of the craft. A 12-scent set gives you enough variety to find which fragrances suit your taste, to try simple blends (lavender and vanilla, cedarwood and orange), and to make personalised gifts. Candle-specific fragrance oils are formulated to bind with wax and throw scent when burned. Do not substitute essential oils or perfume, as they behave differently and can cause fire safety issues.

products.name: Digital Thermometer Probe
products.description: A digital probe thermometer with instant reading, measuring from -50°C to 300°C. Folds for storage. Works for both wax temperature and ambient temperature checking.
products.why_we_picked: Temperature is the most important variable in candle making and the main reason candles fail. Soy wax needs to be around 65°C when you add fragrance (too hot and it evaporates; too cool and it doesn't bind), and poured at around 55–60°C for a smooth finish. A reliable thermometer removes the guesswork. If your starter kit doesn't include one, this is the first additional purchase to make. A basic probe thermometer prevents a lot of wasted wax.

kits.label: Level Up Kit
kits.description: Better containers, scales, and a wax melter for a more consistent and efficient setup.

products.name: Amber Glass Candle Jars with Lids (Set of 12)
products.description: Straight-sided 200ml amber glass jars with aluminium lids. Suitable for soy, coconut, or paraffin wax. The amber glass gives a premium finish and protects fragrance oils from UV degradation.
products.why_we_picked: The container makes a huge difference to how a finished candle looks and burns. Amber glass jars have become the standard for artisan candles because they look professional, keep the fragrance fresh, and give a warm glow when lit. Selling or gifting candles in tins is fine for beginners but glass jars with lids feel premium. Buying a set of 12 keeps your per-unit cost down and gives enough for a proper batch.

products.name: Digital Kitchen Scales (0.1g Precision)
products.description: A compact kitchen scale measuring up to 5kg in 0.1g increments, with a tare function and flat weighing surface. Runs on AAA batteries.
products.why_we_picked: Candle making is more chemistry than cooking: consistent results depend on accurate measurements, especially the fragrance load percentage. Measuring wax and fragrance by weight (not volume) is the professional approach: different fragrance oils have different densities, so measuring by volume introduces errors. A scale that reads to 0.1g lets you hit precise fragrance loads every time, which means predictable scent throw and no wasted materials.

checklist: Soy wax flakes (GW464 or similar container wax)
Pre-tabbed cotton wicks sized for your container diameter
Wick stickers and wick centring bars
Newspaper or baking paper to protect work surfaces

faqs.question: What type of wax is best for beginners?
faqs.answer: Soy wax is the best starting point. It melts at a relatively low temperature (around 50–60°C), is forgiving of minor temperature errors, has good fragrance retention, and cleans up easily with warm soapy water. The most widely recommended variety is Golden Brands 464, which is designed specifically for container candles and is what most beginner tutorials are based on. Paraffin wax has a stronger scent throw but produces more soot; coconut wax gives a beautiful finish but is pricier; beeswax is natural and long-burning but expensive and tricky for scenting.

faqs.question: How do I choose the right wick size?
faqs.answer: Wick size is matched to the diameter of your container, not the height. Too small a wick and the candle tunnels (leaves unmelted wax around the edges); too large and it mushrooms, soots, and burns dangerously hot. Wick manufacturers publish sizing guides matched to specific waxes. Always use these as a starting point. Then test: burn a candle for two hours and check whether the melt pool has reached the edges of the container. It should reach edge-to-edge within about two hours of the first burn. Adjust wick size up or down based on what you see.

faqs.question: Why does my candle have a rough or bumpy top?
faqs.answer: Rough tops (sometimes called 'sinkholes' or 'frosting') are common with soy wax and usually caused by the wax cooling too quickly or being poured too cool. Try pouring at a slightly higher temperature (around 55–60°C for GW464) and leaving the candles to cool slowly at room temperature, away from draughts. A second pour to top up any sink holes that develop in the centre is normal practice. Gentle frosting (a white powdery effect) is natural to soy wax and doesn't affect burn quality.

faqs.question: How much fragrance oil should I add?
faqs.answer: The standard fragrance load for soy container wax is 8–10% of the wax weight. So for 100g of wax, use 8–10g of fragrance oil. More than 10% and the excess fragrance can't bind to the wax, which causes seeping (oil pooling on the surface), poor burn quality, and potentially a fire hazard. Always measure by weight on kitchen scales rather than volume. Check your specific fragrance oil's recommended load, as some are more concentrated than others.

faqs.question: How long should I wait before burning a new candle?
faqs.answer: Soy wax candles benefit from a cure time of 24–48 hours minimum, and ideally 1–2 weeks. During curing the fragrance continues to bind to the wax, improving scent throw. A candle that smells underwhelming fresh off the pour may smell significantly stronger after a week. If you're testing your candles, cure them for at least 48 hours before lighting. If you're making them to sell or give as gifts, aim for a full week's curing time.

faqs.question: Why doesn't my candle smell strongly when it's burning?
faqs.answer: Weak hot throw (scent when burning) is the most common problem in beginner candles. The most likely causes: insufficient fragrance load (try 10% rather than 8%), fragrance added at too high a temperature (above 70°C causes fragrance to evaporate before the wax sets), insufficient cure time (cure for at least a week), or the wick being too small to create a full melt pool. Try increasing fragrance percentage first, then check cure time, then wick size. Some fragrance oils simply have weaker throw than others: citrus scents are notoriously lighter than warm bakery or woody scents.

faqs.question: Can I sell homemade candles in the UK?
faqs.answer: Yes, but there are legal requirements. Candles sold commercially in the UK must comply with the General Product Safety Regulations and the Cosmetic Products (Safety) Regulations where applicable. Practically, this means: performing a CLP (Classification, Labelling and Packaging) risk assessment for each fragrance oil blend and labelling your candles accordingly, keeping records, and ensuring your products are safe to burn. The UK Soy Candle Makers community and CTPA website have free guidance. If you're selling at craft fairs or online, start small, research the requirements, and consider product liability insurance.

faqs.question: What's the safest way to melt wax at home?
faqs.answer: Always use a double boiler method: place your wax in a metal or heat-safe pouring jug, then place the jug in a pan of simmering water. Never melt wax directly in a saucepan on the heat: it can overheat, flashpoint, and ignite. Don't leave melting wax unattended. Use a thermometer and remove from heat as soon as the wax reaches the target temperature. Keep a metal lid or baking sheet nearby to smother a wax fire. Never use water on a wax fire. Have a fire extinguisher accessible in your kitchen.
